State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814
107 SOUTH BROADWAY • • • SUITE 2140A • • • LOS ANGELES, 90012

November 8, 1976

Thomas F. Winfield III
Ebben and Brown
615 South Flower Street
Suite 1201
Los Angeles, California 90017

Dear Mr. Winfield:

Thank you for your letter of October 5, 1976, in which you
requested a review of the activities of your clients, Dr. Bert
Keilen and Dr. Paul Gundlach, and advice as to (i) whether or not
their activities require registration as lobbyists, and (ii)
assuming such activities do not require registration, whether or
not it would be proper for them to withdraw their registration
pursuant to 2 Cal. Admin. Code Section 18601.

2 Cal. Admin. Code Section 18239 delineates four tests to
determine whether or not an individual (who is not an employee
or an official of a local governmental agency) is a lobbyist.
Essentially, these tests are as follows:

1. The individual spends forty hours for which compensation is received, of which at least ten hours are in
direct communication, during any two consecutive calendar
months influencing legislative or administrative action.

2. The individual receives or becomes entitled to
receive $1,000 or more in any 30 day period for the purpose
of communicating directly with legislative, administrative
or state elective officials.

3. The individual spends, in any two consecutive
calendar months, 200 hours engaging in public testimony.

4. The individual spends, in any two consecutive
calendar months, 40 hours engaging in public testimony
and at least one hour of direct communication which is
not public testimony with officials of the agency to whom
the public testimony is directed.

SACRAMENTO (916) 322-5660 • LOS ANGELES (213) 620-5196

FPC
Nov 15 ll 27 Apr 76

Thomas F. Winfield III
November 8, 1976
Page 2


If your clients have never met the tests outlined in 2 Cal.
Admin. Code Section 18239, then they may withdraw their regis-
trations as lobbyists by filing a Notice of Withdrawal with the
Secretary of State and providing the information required by
2 Cal. Admin. Code Section 18601(b).

However, if it is determined that your clients did meet one
or more of the tests as lobbyists, but they no longer meet any of
those tests, then they may file a Notice of Termination (Form 640)
with the Secretary of State.

Please contact me if you have additional questions or if I
may be of further assistance.

Sincerely,

John C. Greenwood

JCG/lkh

Enclosures (2)

cc: Richard Eichman


LAW OFFICES

EBBEN AND BROWN
615 SOUTH FLOWER STREET, SUITE 1201
LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90017

LEO G. EBBEN
TIMOTHY JAY MILLER
J. KENNETH BROWN
THOMAS F. WINFIELD III
ANTHONY CANZONERI
MICHAEL A. LADNA

TELEPHONE (213) 624-1001

ORANGE COUNTY OFFICE
1530 UNION BANK BUILDING
RIO NEWPORT CENTER DRIVE
NEWPORT BEACH, CALIFORNIA 92660
TELEPHONE (714) 644-4181

October 5, 1976

Mr. John C. Greenwood
Fair Political Practices Commission
107 South Broadway
Room 2140a
Los Angeles, California 90012

Re: Withdrawal from Lobbyist Status -
    Bert Keilin, Phd.
    Paul Gundlach, Phd.

Dear Mr. Greenwood:

     My clients, Dr. Bert Keilin and Dr. Paul Gundlach,
have both registered as Lobbyists under the provisions of
the Political Reform Act of 1972.

     After reviewing their activities as set forth in
Dr. Keilin's letter to me of September 27, 1976 (attached
hereto as Exhibit A) it would appear that their activities
in dealing with the State Water Resources Control Board and
the several Regional Water Quality Control Boards are not
involved with quasi-legislative administrative action within
the meaning of Section 18202 of the regulations of the Commission.

     Since the activities of these two gentlemen in otherwise
dealing with legislative or administrative actions fails
to meet any of the tests requiring registration and reporting
under the Act, it would appear that they are not now, nor
have they ever been, lobbyists within the contemplation of
the Act.

     Would you please be so kind as to review the activities
of Doctors Keilin and Gundlach as set forth in Exhibit A hereto and advise them, through me (pursuant to Section 18329
of the Regulations), as to (i) whether such activities require
their registration as lobbyists, and (ii) assuming such activities do not require registration, would it be proper for


Mr. John C. Greenwood
Fair Political Practices Commission
October 5, 1976
Page 2


them to now withdraw their registration under the authority
of Section 18801 of the Regulations.

     Thank you for your kind assistance in this matter.
Should you require any additional information, please do not
hesitate to call.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [illegible]
                                        THOMAS F. WINFIELD III


TFW/pla

Enclosure
CC. Mr. Richard Eichman
     Fair Political Practices Commission
     Sacramento


SEP 30 1976
.TFW

Pacific Water Conditioning Association, Inc.

2041 BUSINESS CENTER DRIVE • IRVINE, CALIFORNIA 92715
SUITE 214
(714) 833-3131

September 27, 1976


Mr. Thomas F. Winfield III
Ebben and Brown
615 South Flower St. #1201
Los Angeles, CA. 90017

Dear Tom:

This letter is to expand upon and clarify the activities of this office
with respect to lobbying activities in the State of California.

Paul F. Gundlach and I registered as lobbyists immediately subsequent
to the passage of proposition 9, with the understanding that our dealings
with administrative agencies in the State required that we do so. At
that time, there was little clarification of which activities would be
classified as lobbying and which activities would not. Furthermore, al-
though we recognize that a large proportion of our activities could not
be classed as lobbying, we wanted to avoid the great difficulty of making
a determination every month regarding the percentage of lobbying activity
which should be reported in terms of dollars and cents. Therefore, we
simply reported all of our salaries and all of our expenses as lobbying-
related. In the light of recent clarifications by the Fair Political
Practices Commission, it appears to us now that we are not now and
never were lobbyists; that the amount of time which we spend in activ-
ities now classified as lobbying must be and are considerably less than
the time limit established.

Both of our activities are quite similar. Our Association is primarily
concerned with the implementation of the Porter-Cologne Act, which concerns
water pollution control in the State of California. Nine regional Water
Quality Control Boards determine appropriate discharge standards for all
dischargers in the State of California. We work with the State Boards
and testify at public hearings in order that proper discharge standards
be established. We often work with the municipalities and water districts
themselves to help determine sources of salinity and potential methods for
reducing salinity of the sewerage treatment effluent. We try to estimate
the impact of water softener discharges in a particular region on the
salinity of the plant effluent and as a whole, on the groundwater itself.
We do a relatively large number of technical studies in this vein. Both
Paul Gundlach and I have Doctorate degrees which relate to the work that
we are doing and much of our input to the Regional Boards, the State
Board, the Municipalities and the Water Districts, are of a highly tech-
nical nature.

DISTRICT 10 OF WATER CONDITIONING ASSOCIATION INTERNATIONAL

ARIZONA CALIFORNIA IDAHO NEVADA

OREGON UTAH WASHINGTON

EXHIBIT A


- 2 -

Mr. Thomas F. Winfield III
Ebben and Brown
Los Angeles, CA.


The remainder of my work is related to my duties as Executive Director
of the Association. I deal in new memberships, conventions, Board
Meetings, educational activities, committee activities, and such.

The Association employs a lobbyist who resides in Sacramento. The
Association now reports as a lobbyist employer and will continue to do so
even if Mr. Gundlach and myself are judged to properly be able to with-
draw our registration. We occasionally accompany our lobbyist in visits
to state legislators in order to support in his efforts on our behalf, but
such visits of mine have averaged less than 2 hours a month in the last
year. Mr. Gundlach's activities along this line have been even less.

I certify that the above description accurately reflects my activities
and it is my belief that interpretations by the Fair Political Practices
Commission enable me now to judge myself not to be a lobbyist. Therefore,
I have provided you with a notice of termination of lobbyist registration
and will abide by the decision of the Fair Political Practices Commission
in giving their advice. Should there be any further questions, I will be
happy to answer them.

Sincerely yours,

PACIFIC WATER CONDITIONING ASSOCIATION, INC.

[signature]
Bert Keilh
Executive Director

BK/rb


LAW OFFICES
EBBEN AND BROWN
615 SOUTH FLOWER STREET, SUITE 1201
LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90017

LEO O. EBBEN
J. KENNETH BROWN
THOMAS C. WILLIAMS
ANTHONY CANZONE
MICHAEL A. LATT

TELEPHONE (213) 624-1001

OF COUNSEL
TIMOTHY JAY MILLER
10 BRISTOL CENTER DRIVE SUITE 1455
NEWPORT BEACH, CALIFORNIA 92660
TELEPHONE (714) 644-0450

November 23, 1976

Mr. John C. Greenwood
Program Coordinator
Fair Political Practices Commission
107 South Broadway
Room 2140a
Los Angeles, California 90012

Re: Withdrawal from Lobbyist Status -
Bert Keilin, Ph.D. and Paul Gundlach Ph.D.
Request for Further and More Specific Advice

Dear Mr. Greenwood:

I have received your letter of November 8, 1976 in
reply to my October 5, 1976 request for advice. Thank you
very much for your prompt response.

In order for me to properly advise my clients, Bert
Keilin and Paul Gundlach, regarding the appropriateness of their
filing a withdrawal of lobbyist registration pursuant to
Cal. Admin. Code §18601, I am requesting further advice.

Recognizing that the appropriate test to determine
whether or not an individual (who is not an employee or an
official of a local government agency) is a lobbyist, is that
four part test set forth in 2 Cal. Admin. Code §18239, as re-
iterated in your letter, the question as to whether Drs. Keilin
and Gundlach are lobbyists under that test can be determined
only after we have decided whether or not time spent in attempt-
ing to influence the several Regional Quality Control Boards
at the time they issue discharge requirements pursuant to
Water Code §13263, and at the time they consider enforcement
of those regulations pursuant to Water Code §13301, constitutes
efforts to influence "administrative action" within the meaning
of the regulations.

I have reviewed both of the Water Code sections just
cited (copies of which are attached to this letter for your
convenience), the provisions of 2 Cal. Admin. Code §18202, which
defines that which is not a "quasi-legislative proceeding" for
the purposes of Government Code §82002, and the Commission


Mr.John C. Greenwood
November 23, 1976
Page 2


Opinions at 1 FPPC Ops. 46 (1975) and 2 FPPC Ops. 57 (1976),
and concluded that:

     (i) The issuance of discharge requirements under
California Water Code, Division 7, Chapter 4, Art. IV,
13260, et seq. constitutes a proceeding involving the
issuance or amendment of a permit, license or other
entitlement for use within the contemplation of 2 Cal.
Admin. Code §18202.

     (ii) The issuance of cease and desist orders by
the Regional Water Quality Control Boards pursuant
to Water Code §13301 constitutes a proceeding to determine
the rights or duties of a person under existing regulations
or policies, or a proceeding to enforce compliance with
existing law, (the previously issued discharge requirements
or to impose sanctions for a violation thereof, both within
the contemplation of Cal. Admin. Code §18202.

     It is my conclusion therefore, that as a matter of law,
the time expended by Drs. Keilin and Gundlach in trying to in-
fluence the content of discharge requirements, or in responding
to proposed cease and desist orders, does not constitute time
which must be computed in determining whether or not they are
lobbyists pursuant to the four tests set forth in my letter
of November 8, 1976. Because of the seriousness of the sanctions
involved, and the desires of my clients to meet the spirit as
well as the letter of the law, I am requesting your specific
advice as to whether time spent in attempting to influence the
contents of waste discharge requirements, or the contents of
cease and desist orders under Water Code Sections 13263 and
13301, respectively, must be considered when determining whether
or not one is a lobbyist under the four tests included in 2
Cal. Admin. Code §18239.

     Thank you in advance for your prompt attention to this
matter.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]

                                        THOMAS F. WINFIELD III
TFW/pla
Enclosure
CC. Richard Eichman


§ 13263. Prescribing requirements; considerations by regional
board

(a) The regional board, after any necessary hearing, shall prescribe requirements as to the nature of any proposed discharge, existing discharge, or material change therein, except discharges into a community sewer system, with relation to the conditions existing from time to time in the disposal area or receiving waters upon or into which the discharge is made or proposed. The requirements shall implement relevant water quality control plans, if any have been adopted, and shall take into consideration the beneficial uses to be protected, the water quality objectives reasonably required for that purpose, other waste discharges, the need to prevent nuisance, and the provisions of Section 13241.

(b) A regional board, in prescribing requirements, need not authorize the utilization of the full waste assimilation capacities of the receiving waters.

(c) The requirements may contain a time schedule, subject to revision in the discretion of the board.

(d) The board may prescribe requirements although no discharge report has been filed.

(e) Upon application by any affected person or on its own motion, the regional board may review and revise requirements. All requirements shall be reviewed periodically.

(f) The regional board shall notify in writing the person making or proposing the discharge or the change therein of the discharge requirements to be met. After receipt of such notice, the person so notified shall provide adequate means to meet such requirements.

(g) No discharge of waste into the waters of the state, whether or not such discharge is made pursuant to waste discharge requirements, shall create a vested right to continue such discharge. All discharges of waste into waters of the state are privileges, not rights.

(Added by Stats.1969, c. 482, p. 1063, § 18, operative Jan. 1, 1970.)

Legislative Committee Comment

As with respect to Section 13260, it is intended that jurisdiction of a regional board under this section be limited to cases where

463 Cal.Gov.—42                657


§ 13301. Cease and desist orders

When a regional board finds that a discharge of waste is taking place or threatening to take place in violation of requirements or discharge prohibitions prescribed by the regional board or the state board, the board may issue an order to cease and desist and direct that those persons not complying with the requirements or discharge prohibitions (a) comply forthwith, (b) comply in accordance with a time schedule set by the board, or (c) in the event of a threatened violation, take appropriate remedial or preventive action. In the event of an existing or threatened violation of waste discharge requirements in the operation of a community sewer system, cease and desist orders may restrict or prohibit the volume, type, or concentration of waste that might be added to such system by dischargers who did not discharge into the system prior to the issuance of the cease and desist order. Cease and desist orders may be issued directly by a board, after notice and hearing, or in accordance with the procedure set forth in Section 13302.

(Amended by Stats.1971, c. 1288, p. 2225, § 10.)

Cease and desist orders, see 23 Cal.Adm. Code 2210 et seq.

1971 Amendment. Added state board.

Supplementary Index to Notes

Findings 3
Notice and hearing 2

2. Notice and hearing

Where notices of regional water quality control board's cease and desist order were sent to each of the sewage dischargers affected by cease and desist order, notices were sent to all interested parties, and that the interested board including interest in hearings, and general notice of hearing was published in local newspaper, failure to

send notice to particular interest group of which board was unaware, was not failure to give proper notice of hearing. Morehead v. California Regional Water Quality Control Bd. (1975) 119 Cal.Rptr. 586, 45 C.A.3d 442.

3. Findings

In action for writ of mandamus to re strain enforcement of cease and desist orders issued by regional water quality control board, trial court's finding that dischargers had sent their own evidence or cross-examine and refute evidence presented in support of board's cease and desist order, as reported by record. Morehead v. California Regional Water Quality Control Bd. (1975) 119 Cal.Rptr. 586, 45 C.A.3d 442.
